factions.name: House of Lancaster
factions.description: The red rose. The ruling house of England for three generations — Henry IV, Henry V, [[Henry VI]]. Their claim derives from John of Gaunt, fourth son of Edward III, but passes through an illegitimate line. Their strength is legitimacy; their weakness is [[Henry VI]] himself — a pious, gentle, occasionally catatonic king who cannot govern. The real Lancastrian force is his queen, [[Margaret of Anjou]], whose ferocity and political will hold the cause together long after the cause should have collapsed. The red rose is finally extinguished at [[Bosworth Field]] in 1485.

log.note: The [[House of Lancaster|Lancastrian]] court loses its chief military commander at St. Albans. [[Margaret of Anjou]], excluded from formal power because she is a woman, begins building her own political network — writing to nobles, controlling access to the king, accumulating influence outside the structures that exclude her. She will not be excluded for long.

The [[House of Lancaster|Lancastrian]] cause after Towton is a rump: a fugitive king, an exile queen, and a handful of northern castles that hold out until 1464. [[Margaret of Anjou]] leads fruitless campaigns from France and Scotland, burning through political capital and receiving less each time. The red rose is not extinguished — but it is very nearly so.

The red rose is finished. Henry VI murdered in the Tower, the Prince of Wales killed at Tewkesbury, Margaret in exile. There is no Lancastrian heir. The cause has no rallying point. The only remaining Lancastrian bloodline passes through Henry Tudor — a fourteen-year-old exile in Brittany with a questionable claim who no one takes seriously yet.

Consequence of House of York (rival): Richard III's usurpation fractures the Yorkist coalition. Lord Hastings — Edward IV's closest friend — is arrested and executed on Richard's orders within minutes, on a council table in the Tower, without trial. The message is clear: loyalty to Edward's memory is a threat. Many former Yorkists begin corresponding with Henry Tudor.

Consequence of House of Tudor (ally): Henry VII marries Elizabeth of York on January 18, 1486 — the union of the red and white roses. He does not adopt the combined Tudor rose as his symbol until the marriage is done: he is careful to make clear he holds the crown by conquest, not by right of his wife. The Tudor dynasty rules England for 118 years. The Wars of the Roses are over.

factions.name: House of York
factions.description: The white rose. Their claim descends from Edward III through two lines — both stronger than [[House of Lancaster|Lancaster's]] if inheritance is traced honestly. [[Richard Duke of York]] asserts this claim not by ambition alone but by the specific failure of Lancastrian governance. His son [[Edward IV]] is everything [[Henry VI]] is not: physically imposing, politically shrewd, militarily devastating. York wins the crown twice. It is a Yorkist internal collapse — Richard III — that ends their dynasty.

log.note: Consequence of House of Lancaster (rival): The [[House of Lancaster|Lancastrian]] court loses its chief military commander at St. Albans. [[Margaret of Anjou]], excluded from formal power because she is a woman, begins building her own political network — writing to nobles, controlling access to the king, accumulating influence outside the structures that exclude her. She will not be excluded for long.

Richard III's usurpation fractures the Yorkist coalition. Lord Hastings — Edward IV's closest friend — is arrested and executed on Richard's orders within minutes, on a council table in the Tower, without trial. The message is clear: loyalty to Edward's memory is a threat. Many former Yorkists begin corresponding with Henry Tudor.

The Tudor dynasty rules England for 118 years. The Wars of the Roses are over.

factions.name: House of Neville
factions.description: The kingmakers. The Nevilles are the most powerful noble family in England — wealthier than most kings, connected to both [[House of York|York]] and [[House of Lancaster|Lancaster]] by blood. [[Richard Neville, Earl of Warwick]], is the central figure: he makes [[Edward IV]] king, then unmakes him, then dies trying to restore [[House of Lancaster|Lancaster]]. His nickname — the Kingmaker — is earned. No single magnate in English history holds more political and military power for longer. His fatal flaw: he cannot accept that the king he made does not need him anymore.

log.note: Consequence of House of York (ally): Richard III's usurpation fractures the Yorkist coalition. Lord Hastings — Edward IV's closest friend — is arrested and executed on Richard's orders within minutes, on a council table in the Tower, without trial. The message is clear: loyalty to Edward's memory is a threat. Many former Yorkists begin corresponding with Henry Tudor.

factions.name: House of Tudor
factions.description: The red dragon. An obscure Welsh dynasty with a tenuous Lancastrian bloodline through an illegitimate line — [[Henry Tudor|Henry Tudor's]] claim is weak on paper. What Henry VII has instead of legitimacy is patience, timing, and [[Kingdom of France|French]] backing. He waits in [[France and Brittany]] for fourteen years. When [[Richard Duke of Gloucester|Richard III's]] support collapses in 1485, he lands in Wales, gathers an army of Welsh and English defectors, and kills Richard at [[Bosworth Field|Bosworth]]. He then marries Elizabeth of York — uniting the roses — and rules for twenty-four years. Every English monarch since has been a Tudor or a Tudor descendant.

log.note: Consequence of House of Lancaster (ally): The [[House of Lancaster|Lancastrian]] court loses its chief military commander at St. Albans. [[Margaret of Anjou]], excluded from formal power because she is a woman, begins building her own political network — writing to nobles, controlling access to the king, accumulating influence outside the structures that exclude her. She will not be excluded for long.

factions.name: Kingdom of France
factions.description: Louis XI — 'the Spider King' — watches the English tear themselves apart with quiet satisfaction. An England consumed by civil war cannot threaten France. Louis funds [[Richard Neville, Earl of Warwick|Warwick]] when [[Richard Neville, Earl of Warwick|Warwick]] switches sides. He harbors [[Henry Tudor]] in exile. He is not England's enemy so much as England's opportunist: wherever the English conflict creates leverage, Louis applies it. His backing of [[House of Tudor|Tudor]] at the critical moment in 1485 is the margin that makes [[Bosworth Field|Bosworth]] possible.

log.note: Consequence of House of Lancaster (ally): The [[House of Lancaster|Lancastrian]] court loses its chief military commander at St. Albans.
